title: Quiz: SIDH and SIKE
description: Test your understanding of SIDH/SIKE design

questions.question: In SIDH, why does Alice publish auxiliary points φ_A(P_B) and φ_A(Q_B)?

options.text: To enable Bob to reconstruct his kernel in E_A

questions.explanation: Bob needs to compute his isogeny starting from E_A. The auxiliary points φ_A(P_B), φ_A(Q_B) tell him where his kernel generators went, allowing him to reconstruct his kernel in E_A.

questions.question: What type of prime is used in SIDH/SIKE?
questions.explanation: SIDH uses primes of the form p = 2^a · 3^b · f - 1 where f is small. This allows efficient computation of both 2-power and 3-power isogenies.

questions.question: What was SIKE's main advantage over other post-quantum KEMs?

options.text: Smallest combined public key and ciphertext size

questions.explanation: SIKE had the smallest combined key+ciphertext size (~676 bytes for Level 1) compared to other candidates. This was traded for slower operations.

questions.question: How does SIKE relate to SIDH?

options.text: SIKE wraps SIDH into a KEM using FO transform

questions.explanation: SIKE (Supersingular Isogeny Key Encapsulation) wraps SIDH into a KEM using the Fujisaki-Okamoto transform for IND-CCA2 security. It didn't fix the underlying vulnerability.

questions.question: Why did Alice use 2-power isogenies and Bob use 3-power isogenies?

options.text: To ensure their kernels are disjoint and the protocol works

questions.explanation: Using coprime degrees (2^a and 3^b) ensures the kernels E₀[2^a] and E₀[3^b] are disjoint. This is essential for the commutative diagram to work.

questions.question: What was the key assumption underlying SIDH's security?

options.text: Finding isogenies between curves is hard

questions.explanation: SIDH's security relied on the difficulty of finding the isogeny between two supersingular curves. However, the auxiliary data made this easier than the generic problem.

questions.question: Approximately how slow was SIKE compared to Kyber?
questions.explanation: SIKE was roughly 100-500x slower than Kyber. KeyGen/Encaps/Decaps took 10-60ms for SIKE vs ~0.1ms for Kyber.

questions.question: SIDH is still considered secure for post-quantum cryptography.
questions.explanation: False! SIDH was completely broken in 2022 by Castryck-Decru and independently by others. The attack runs in polynomial time on a classical computer.
